evidence_citations.excerpt: I feel like I'm doing this for myself and not for her. The nightmares keep me up, and I keep putting off the work because I don't know how to channel the grief into the design.
I keep thinking the site should be perfect, but every time I start I feel like I'm failing her.
I feel like I'm letting her down if I make a mistake.
I still feel like I'm not doing enough, and the nightmares keep me up.

transcript.text: Every time I sit down to sketch her memorial site, I feel like I'm doing this for myself and not for her. The nightmares keep me up, and I keep putting off the work because I don't know how to channel the grief into the design. My career feels like a dead end without her to share it with.

The memorial site project feels overwhelming, and it's like the grief is getting in the way of your creativity. You're used to sharing your accomplishments with your loved one, and now that's not possible. It's understandable that you'd question whether you're doing this project for yourself or for her. Can you tell me more about what you hope to achieve with this memorial site, and how you think it could help you honor her memory?

I keep thinking the site should be perfect, but every time I start I feel like I'm failing her. The nightmares keep me up and I just keep putting it off. I don't even know if I should keep going at all—feels like my whole career is stuck.

You're putting a lot of pressure on yourself to create the perfect memorial site, and it's clear that the fear of not doing it justice is really holding you back. The nightmares and the feeling of being stuck in your career are all connected to the grief you're still navigating. It might be helpful to explore what 'perfect' means to you in the context of this project, and how that's impacting your ability to move forward. What do you think is the most important aspect of the memorial site that you want to get right?

Honestly, I keep drifting back to the idea that the site has to embody her whole life, and that thought just freezes me. The dreams keep me awake, and I keep postponing the design because I can't see how to make it feel true. I don't know if I'm doing this for her or just to fill the void in my work.

The weight of capturing her entire life in this memorial site is really daunting for you. You're used to sharing your creative work with her, and now that's not possible, making it even harder to move forward. It sounds like the fear of not doing it justice is a major barrier. Can you tell me more about what specific aspects of her life you feel are essential to include in the design?

I keep thinking the music she loved should be the centerpiece, because that was her escape. But even that feels too big to capture and I keep putting it off, watching my nightmares keep me up all night.

The music she loved was her escape, and now it feels like a daunting task to make it the centerpiece of the memorial site. It's like you're trying to capture the essence of her through this one element, and that's a lot of pressure. It makes sense that you're putting it off, given how overwhelming it feels. Yeah, it's tough to see how to make it feel true to her memory, especially when the nightmares are still keeping you up at night. Can you think of any small way to start incorporating her music into the design, just to get started?

I tried adding a playlist, but it still feels like a huge step. The nightmares keep coming, and every time I open my laptop I just stare at the blank page. I wonder if pushing forward will make it any less overwhelming.

You took a step by adding a playlist, which is a great way to start incorporating her music into the design. I can see how it still feels like a huge step, and the nightmares are still affecting you. It's understandable that you're wondering if pushing forward will make it any less overwhelming. Can you tell me more about what's holding you back from opening your laptop and starting to work on the design?

I keep staring at the screen and thinking about how much I want to do this right, but the nightmares keep creeping in and I feel like I'm letting her down if I make a mistake. It feels like I'm stuck, and I don't know if I even want to keep going with the site or my work.

The pressure to get this memorial site just right is really weighing on you, and it's like the nightmares are a constant reminder of your fear of not doing it justice. You're trying to honor her memory, but it feels like you're stuck and unsure if you're doing it for her or for yourself. It's understandable that you're questioning whether to keep going with the site or your work, given how overwhelming it all feels. Yeah, it's a lot to carry on your own. What's the smallest part of the project that you feel like you can tackle right now, without feeling like you're letting her down?

I keep thinking the simplest thing is just to drop a photo of the two of us on the homepage, but the nightmares make me keep staring at the screen and not actually doing it. Maybe I should just add that one image instead of the whole site, but I still feel guilty for not doing more. I just want to feel like I'm doing something, even if it's tiny.

The idea of just adding a photo of the two of you to the homepage feels like a tiny step, but it's still really hard to take that step when the nightmares are haunting you. It's like the guilt of not doing more is holding you back, even though a part of you just wants to feel like you're doing something, anything, to honor her memory. Yeah, it's a lot to navigate, and it makes sense that you're feeling stuck. Adding that one image might be a way to start, even if it doesn't feel like enough right now.

I actually dropped a photo on the homepage, but the nightmares keep me up and I still feel like I'm not doing enough. It's hard to see how this small act can mean anything when everything feels stuck.

You've taken a step by adding that photo to the homepage, and that's something to acknowledge, even if it doesn't feel like enough right now. The nightmares are still haunting you, and it's understandable that you're struggling to see how this small act can mean anything when everything feels stuck. It's like you're trying to find a way to honor her memory, but the guilt and pressure are making it hard to move forward. Yeah, it's a tough spot to be in.

I know I did put the photo, but I still feel like I'm not doing enough, and the nightmares keep me up. I keep wondering if this is even worth it, especially with my career feeling like a dead end without her.

You've taken a step by adding that photo, and that's something to acknowledge. The nightmares are still haunting you, and it's understandable that you're struggling to see how this small act can mean anything when everything feels stuck. Your career feeling like a dead end without her is a heavy burden to carry. Can you think of any way your creativity, which is so important to you, might help you find a sense of purpose or meaning in your work right now?

I don't know if I can find a way to turn this into something useful. I think maybe I could design a small piece that shows the moments we shared, but the nightmares keep me up and I keep putting it off. I keep feeling stuck in my career and like I'm not making progress. It feels like I'm just stuck in a loop of guilt and fear.

You're feeling really stuck right now, and it's like the guilt and fear are looping back on you, making it hard to see a way forward with your design and career. Adding that photo to the homepage was a step, but the nightmares and feelings of not doing enough are still weighing on you. It's understandable that you're questioning whether you can find a way to turn this into something useful. What's the smallest part of this design piece that you feel like you can tackle right now, without feeling overwhelmed by the whole project?

turn_traces.user_message: Every time I sit down to sketch her memorial site, I feel like I'm doing this for myself and not for her. The nightmares keep me up, and I keep putting off the work because I don't know how to channel the grief into the design. My career feels like a dead end without her to share it with.

common_candidates.label: Support
common_candidates.what: Sympathetic / understanding statement
common_candidates.transition_fn: Sustains engagement at any stage; especially during distress / resistance

common_candidates.label: Facilitate
common_candidates.what: Brief encouragement to continue ('mm-hmm', 'go on')
common_candidates.transition_fn: Sustains conversation flow at any stage

common_candidates.label: Complex Reflection
common_candidates.what: Reflect with added inferred meaning, depth, or shift in emphasis
common_candidates.transition_fn: Develops discrepancy → drives precontemplation → contemplation

common_candidates.label: Reframe
common_candidates.what: Offer alternative interpretation that widens or shifts meaning
common_candidates.transition_fn: Widens stuck frame → drives precontemplation → contemplation

common_candidates.label: Inform (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Provide information after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Builds awareness (pre → contempl) or supports informed decision (contempl → prep)

common_candidates.label: Raise Concern (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Voice concern about a plan or pattern after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Develops discrepancy gently (pre → contempl) or surfaces risks to refine plan (prep → action)

common_candidates.label: Evoke
common_candidates.what: Invite client's own reasons for change in their words (counselor-side mirror of MISC client 'Change Talk')
common_candidates.transition_fn: Core MI move for resolving ambivalence → drives contemplation → preparation

common_candidates.label: Closed Question
common_candidates.what: Question that limits answer to yes/no/short — used sparingly
common_candidates.transition_fn: Confirms commitment or specific plan elements (prep → action)

common_candidates.label: Advise (with permission)
common_candidates.what: Suggest a course of action after asking permission
common_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action; supports change planning

common_candidates.label: Structure
common_candidates.what: Explain what will happen next in the session or turn
common_candidates.transition_fn: Drives preparation → action by structuring the change plan

The evidence that stands out is your difficulty in channeling grief into your design and the sense of emptiness in your career without your loved one to share it with. My entry point will be to acknowledge the pain of trying to create a memorial site while feeling like you're doing it for yourself, and how that's affecting your ability to move forward with the project.
